verses.text: Jehu writes to Samaria's leaders, daring them to set up one of Ahab's seventy sons to fight him; terrified, they submit and offer to do whatever he says. The mighty house collapses without a battle. When God's judgment is set, the props of a doomed dynasty give way at a word.

Jehu writes to Samaria's leaders, daring them to set up one of Ahab's seventy sons to fight him; terrified, they submit and offer to do whatever he says. The mighty house collapses without a battle. When God's judgment is set, the props of a doomed dynasty give way at a word.

Jehu writes to Samaria's leaders, daring them to set up one of Ahab's seventy sons to fight him; terrified, they submit and offer to do whatever he says. The mighty house collapses without a battle. When God's judgment is set, the props of a doomed dynasty give way at a word.

Jehu writes to Samaria's leaders, daring them to set up one of Ahab's seventy sons to fight him; terrified, they submit and offer to do whatever he says. The mighty house collapses without a battle. When God's judgment is set, the props of a doomed dynasty give way at a word.

At Jehu's command the rulers behead all seventy of Ahab's sons and send the heads in baskets. The slaughter is grim but fulfills God's sentence on a bloodstained house. The sins of Ahab and Jezebel reap a terrible, exact harvest in their offspring.

At Jehu's command the rulers behead all seventy of Ahab's sons and send the heads in baskets. The slaughter is grim but fulfills God's sentence on a bloodstained house. The sins of Ahab and Jezebel reap a terrible, exact harvest in their offspring.

At Jehu's command the rulers behead all seventy of Ahab's sons and send the heads in baskets. The slaughter is grim but fulfills God's sentence on a bloodstained house. The sins of Ahab and Jezebel reap a terrible, exact harvest in their offspring.

Jehu addresses the people: he conspired, but who killed all these? — spreading the guilt to the leaders too, then destroys all that remained of Ahab's house. The judgment is thorough. God's reckoning with persistent evil leaves no remnant of it to revive.

Jehu addresses the people: he conspired, but who killed all these? — spreading the guilt to the leaders too, then destroys all that remained of Ahab's house. The judgment is thorough. God's reckoning with persistent evil leaves no remnant of it to revive.

Jehu addresses the people: he conspired, but who killed all these? — spreading the guilt to the leaders too, then destroys all that remained of Ahab's house. The judgment is thorough. God's reckoning with persistent evil leaves no remnant of it to revive.

On the road Jehu meets Ahaziah's relatives and has all forty-two slain. The purge widens to the allied house of Judah. Those who bound themselves to Ahab's family by blood and friendship share its fall; evil alliances prove costly in the day of judgment.

Jehu finds Jehonadab, takes him up to 'see my zeal for the LORD,' and finishes off Ahab's house in Samaria. He is glad to have a witness to his fervor. Yet zeal eager for an audience may be mixed with motives less pure than it claims.

Jehu finds Jehonadab, takes him up to 'see my zeal for the LORD,' and finishes off Ahab's house in Samaria. He is glad to have a witness to his fervor. Yet zeal eager for an audience may be mixed with motives less pure than it claims.

Jehu finds Jehonadab, takes him up to 'see my zeal for the LORD,' and finishes off Ahab's house in Samaria. He is glad to have a witness to his fervor. Yet zeal eager for an audience may be mixed with motives less pure than it claims.

Jehu cunningly proclaims a great feast for Baal, vowing to serve him more than Ahab did, to gather every worshiper in one place. He uses deceit to trap the idolaters. His aim is right, but craft and bloodshed are the tools he chooses to reach it.

Jehu cunningly proclaims a great feast for Baal, vowing to serve him more than Ahab did, to gather every worshiper in one place. He uses deceit to trap the idolaters. His aim is right, but craft and bloodshed are the tools he chooses to reach it.

Jehu cunningly proclaims a great feast for Baal, vowing to serve him more than Ahab did, to gather every worshiper in one place. He uses deceit to trap the idolaters. His aim is right, but craft and bloodshed are the tools he chooses to reach it.

All the worshipers of Baal fill the temple, and Jehu makes sure no servant of the LORD is among them. The net is drawn tight around the idolaters. Their devotion to a false god gathers them, unknowing, to their own destruction.

All the worshipers of Baal fill the temple, and Jehu makes sure no servant of the LORD is among them. The net is drawn tight around the idolaters. Their devotion to a false god gathers them, unknowing, to their own destruction.

All the worshipers of Baal fill the temple, and Jehu makes sure no servant of the LORD is among them. The net is drawn tight around the idolaters. Their devotion to a false god gathers them, unknowing, to their own destruction.

When the offerings are made, Jehu's eighty men fall on the worshipers and slay them all. The judgment on organized Baal-worship is swift and total. The sin that had captured a nation is, in a single stroke, cut out of it.

When the offerings are made, Jehu's eighty men fall on the worshipers and slay them all. The judgment on organized Baal-worship is swift and total. The sin that had captured a nation is, in a single stroke, cut out of it.

They burn Baal's images, break down his temple, and make it a latrine; 'thus Jehu destroyed Baal out of Israel.' The idol is utterly defiled and abolished. God uses even a ruthless king to sweep away the abomination that defiled His people.

They burn Baal's images, break down his temple, and make it a latrine; 'thus Jehu destroyed Baal out of Israel.' The idol is utterly defiled and abolished. God uses even a ruthless king to sweep away the abomination that defiled His people.

They burn Baal's images, break down his temple, and make it a latrine; 'thus Jehu destroyed Baal out of Israel.' The idol is utterly defiled and abolished. God uses even a ruthless king to sweep away the abomination that defiled His people.

Yet Jehu does not turn from Jeroboam's golden calves, nor take heed to walk in God's law with all his heart. His reform stops short of his own convenient sins. Zeal against others' idols means little while we cling to the idols that suit ourselves.

Yet Jehu does not turn from Jeroboam's golden calves, nor take heed to walk in God's law with all his heart. His reform stops short of his own convenient sins. Zeal against others' idols means little while we cling to the idols that suit ourselves.

In those days God begins to cut Israel short, and Hazael presses them hard; Jehu reigns twenty-eight years, then dies. Even the executioner of judgment, unrepentant at heart, comes under the shadow of decline. Partial obedience secures no lasting blessing.
